infobox.value: * [St. Constantius](/wiki/Constantius_of_Perugia "Constantius of Perugia") * [St. Herculanus](/wiki/Herculanus_of_Perugia "Herculanus of Perugia") * [St. Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Rome "Lawrence of Rome")

([/pəˈruːdʒə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*pə-ROO-jə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/-dʒiə, peɪˈ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠jee-ə, pay-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[5]](#cite_note-5) Italian: [[peˈruːdʒa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Perugia.ogg "File:It-Perugia.ogg"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Perusia*) is the capital city of [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria") and the [province of Perugia](/wiki/Province_of_Perugia "Province of Perugia") in central Italy, crossed by the River [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber"). The city is located about 164 km (102 mi) north of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and 148 km (92 mi) southeast of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"). It covers a high hilltop and part of the valleys around the area. It has 162,467 inhabitants as of 2025.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

**Perugia** ([/pəˈruːdʒə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*pə-ROO-jə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/-dʒiə, peɪˈ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠jee-ə, pay-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[5]](#cite_note-5) Italian: [[peˈruːdʒa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Perugia.ogg "File:It-Perugia.ogg"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Perusia*) is the capital city of [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria") and the [province of Perugia](/wiki/Province_of_Perugia "Province of Perugia") in central Italy, crossed by the River [Tiber](/wiki/Tiber "Tiber"). The city is located about 164 km (102 mi) north of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and 148 km (92 mi) southeast of [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"). It covers a high hilltop and part of the valleys around the area. It has 162,467 inhabitants as of 2025.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

The history of Perugia dates back to the Etruscan period, with Perugia serving as one of the main [Etruscan cities](/wiki/Etruscan_cities "Etruscan cities").

The city is also known as a [university](/wiki/University "University") town, with the [University of Perugia](/wiki/University_of_Perugia "University of Perugia") founded in 1308, the [University for Foreigners](/wiki/University_for_Foreigners_Perugia "University for Foreigners Perugia"), and some smaller colleges such as the Academy of Fine Arts "Pietro Vannucci" ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Accademia di Belle Arti "Pietro Vannucci"*) public athenaeum founded in 1573, the Perugia University Institute of Linguistic Mediation for translators and interpreters, and the Music Conservatory of Perugia founded in 1788.

Perugia is also a well-known cultural and artistic centre of Italy. The city hosts multiple annual festivals and events, such as the [Umbria Jazz Festival](/wiki/Umbria_Jazz_Festival "Umbria Jazz Festival") in July, and the [International Journalism Festival](/wiki/International_Journalism_Festival "International Journalism Festival") in April, and is associated with multiple notable people in the arts.

Painter Pietro Vannucci, nicknamed [Perugino](/wiki/Perugino "Perugino"), was a native of [Città della Pieve](/wiki/Citt%C3%A0_della_Pieve "Città della Pieve"), near Perugia. He decorated the local *Sala del Cambio* with a series of [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"), and eight of his pictures can be seen in the [National Gallery of Umbria](/wiki/Galleria_Nazionale_dell%27Umbria "Galleria Nazionale dell'Umbria").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Perugino may have been the teacher of [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) the great [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") artist who produced five paintings in Perugia (today no longer in the city)[[8]](#cite_note-8) and one fresco.[[9]](#cite_note-severo-9) Another painter, [Pinturicchio](/wiki/Pinturicchio "Pinturicchio"), lived in Perugia. [Galeazzo Alessi](/wiki/Galeazzo_Alessi "Galeazzo Alessi") is the most famous architect from Perugia.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The city's symbol is the [griffin](/wiki/Griffin "Griffin"), which can be seen in the form of [plaques](/wiki/Commemorative_plaque "Commemorative plaque") and [statues](/wiki/Statue "Statue") on buildings around the city. It is also the symbol of the local football club [A.C. Perugia](/wiki/A.C._Perugia "A.C. Perugia"), who have previously played in the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"). Having never been Italian champions, the club went unbeaten in the [1978–79](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_Serie_A "1978–79 Serie A") season despite finishing second in the championship.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Perugia](/wiki/Timeline_of_Perugia "Timeline of Perugia").

### Umbrians and Etruscans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Umbrians and Etruscans")]

Perugia was an [Umbrian](/wiki/Umbri "Umbri") settlement[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-11) but first appears in written history as *[Perusia](/wiki/Perusia "Perusia")*, one of the 12 [confederate](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization#Etruscan_League "Etruscan civilization") cities of [Etruria](/wiki/Etruria "Etruria");[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-11) it was first mentioned in [Q. Fabius Pictor's](/wiki/Quintus_Fabius_Pictor "Quintus Fabius Pictor") account, used by [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy"), of the expedition carried out against the [Etruscan League](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization#Etruscan_League "Etruscan civilization") by [Fabius Maximus Rullianus](/wiki/Fabius_Maximus_Rullianus "Fabius Maximus Rullianus")[[12]](#cite_note-12) in 310 or 309 BC. At that time a thirty-year *[indutiae](/wiki/Truce "Truce")* (truce) was agreed upon;[[13]](#cite_note-13) however, in 295 Perusia took part in the [Third Samnite War](/wiki/Samnite_Wars "Samnite Wars") and was forced, with Volsinii and Arretium ([Arezzo](/wiki/Arezzo "Arezzo")), to sue for peace in the following year.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Roman period")]

In 216 and 205 BC, it assisted Rome in the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War"), but afterwards it is not mentioned until 41–40 BC, when [Lucius Antonius](/wiki/Lucius_Antonius_(brother_of_Mark_Antony) "Lucius Antonius (brother of Mark Antony)") took refuge there, and was defeated by [Octavian](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") after a long siege, and its senators sent to their deaths. A number of [lead bullets used by slingers](/wiki/Sling_(weapon)#Ammunition "Sling (weapon)") have been found in and around the city.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The city was burnt, we are told, with the exception of the temples of [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan_(mythology) "Vulcan (mythology)") and [Juno](/wiki/Juno_(mythology) "Juno (mythology)")—the massive Etruscan terrace-walls,[[16]](#cite_note-16) naturally, can hardly have suffered at all—and the town, with the territory for a mile round, was allowed to be occupied by whoever chose. It must have been rebuilt almost at once, for several bases for statues exist, inscribed *Augusto sacr(um) Perusia restituta*; but it did not become a *[colonia](/wiki/Colonia_(Roman) "Colonia (Roman)")*, until 251–253 AD, when it was resettled as *Colonia Vibia Augusta Perusia*, under the emperor [Trebonianus Gallus](/wiki/Trebonianus_Gallus "Trebonianus Gallus").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Early Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early Middle Ages")]

It is hardly mentioned except by the geographers until it was the only city in Umbria to resist [Totila](/wiki/Totila "Totila") and the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), who captured it and laid the city waste in 547, after a long siege, apparently after the city's Byzantine garrison evacuated. Negotiations with the besieging forces fell to the city's bishop, [Herculanus](/wiki/Herculanus_of_Perugia "Herculanus of Perugia"), as representative of the townspeople.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Totila is said to have ordered the bishop to be [flayed](/wiki/Flayed "Flayed") and beheaded. St. Herculanus (Sant'Ercolano) later became the city's [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

In the [Lombard](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") period, Perugia is spoken of as one of the principal cities of [Tuscia](/wiki/Tuscia "Tuscia").[[20]](#cite_note-20) In the 9th century, with the consent of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") and [Louis the Pious](/wiki/Louis_the_Pious "Louis the Pious"), it passed under the popes; but by the 11th century, its [commune](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") was asserting itself, and for many centuries the city continued to maintain an independent life, warring against many of the neighbouring lands and cities— [Foligno](/wiki/Foligno "Foligno"), [Assisi](/wiki/Assisi "Assisi"), [Spoleto](/wiki/Spoleto "Spoleto"), [Todi](/wiki/Todi "Todi"), [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"), [Arezzo](/wiki/Arezzo "Arezzo"), etc. In 1186, [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry VI, Holy Roman Emperor"), *rex romanorum* and future emperor, granted diplomatic recognition to the [consular](/wiki/Consul "Consul") government of the city; afterward, [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III"), whose major aim was to give state dignity to the dominions having been constituting the [patrimony of St. Peter](/wiki/Patrimonium_Sancti_Petri "Patrimonium Sancti Petri"), acknowledged the validity of the imperial statement and recognised the established civic practices as having the force of law.[[21]](#cite_note-commune-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/01_Perugia_Grifo_Codice_Medioevo.jpg/250px-01_Perugia_Grifo_Codice_Medioevo.jpg)](/wiki/File:01_Perugia_Grifo_Codice_Medioevo.jpg)

Perugia [griffin](/wiki/Griffin "Griffin"), in a medieval [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [document](/wiki/Document "Document")

On various occasions, the popes found asylum from the tumults of Rome within its walls, and it was the meetingplace of five [conclaves](/wiki/Papal_conclave "Papal conclave") ([Perugia Papacy](/wiki/Perugia_Papacy "Perugia Papacy")), including those that elected [Honorius III](/wiki/Pope_Honorius_III "Pope Honorius III") (1216), [Clement IV](/wiki/Pope_Clement_IV "Pope Clement IV") (1265), [Celestine V](/wiki/Pope_Celestine_V "Pope Celestine V") (1294), and [Clement V](/wiki/Pope_Clement_V "Pope Clement V") (1305); the papal presence was characterised by a pacificatory rule between the internal rivalries.[[21]](#cite_note-commune-21) But Perugia had no mind simply to subserve the papal interests and never accepted papal sovereignty; the city used to exercise a jurisdiction over the members of the clergy, moreover in 1282, Perugia was excommunicated due to a new military offensive against the Ghibellines regardless of a papal prohibition. On the other hand, side by side with the 13th-century bronze griffin of Perugia above the door of the [Palazzo dei Priori](/wiki/Palazzo_dei_Priori "Palazzo dei Priori") stands, as a Guelphic emblem, the [lion](/wiki/Lion_(heraldry) "Lion (heraldry)"), and Perugia remained loyal for the most part to the Guelph party in the struggles of [Guelphs and Ghibellines](/wiki/Guelphs_and_Ghibellines "Guelphs and Ghibellines"), but this dominant tendency was rather an anti-Germanic and Italian political strategy.[[21]](#cite_note-commune-21) The [Angevin](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Anjou "Capetian House of Anjou") presence in Italy appeared to offer a counterpoise to papal powers; in 1319, Perugia declared the Angevin Saint [Louis of Toulouse](/wiki/Louis_of_Toulouse "Louis of Toulouse") "Protector of the city's sovereignty and of the Palazzo of its Priors"[[22]](#cite_note-22) and set his figure among the other patron saints above the rich doorway of the Palazzo dei Priori. Midway through the 14th century [Bartholus of Sassoferrato](/wiki/Bartholus_of_Sassoferrato "Bartholus of Sassoferrato"), who was a renowned jurist, asserted that Perugia was dependent upon neither imperial nor papal support.[[21]](#cite_note-commune-21) In 1347, at the time of [Rienzi's](/wiki/Cola_di_Rienzi "Cola di Rienzi") unfortunate enterprise in reviving the Roman republic, Perugia sent 10 ambassadors to pay him honour, and when papal legates sought to coerce it by foreign soldiers, or to exact contributions, they met with vigorous resistance, which broke into open warfare with [Pope Urban V](/wiki/Pope_Urban_V "Pope Urban V") in 1369; in 1370, the noble party reached an agreement signing the [treaty of Bologna](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bologna "Treaty of Bologna"), and Perugia was forced to accept a papal legate; however. the vicar-general of the Papal States, [Gérard du Puy](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_du_Puy "Gérard du Puy"), Abbot of Marmoutier and nephew of [Gregory IX](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_IX "Pope Gregory IX"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) was expelled by a popular uprising in 1375, and his fortification of Porta Sole was razed to the ground.[[24]](#cite_note-touring-24)

##### [Biordo Michelotti](/wiki/Biordo_Michelotti "Biordo Michelotti") (1393–1398)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Biordo Michelotti (1393–1398)")]

On August 5, 1393, the condottiero (mercenary captain) Biordo Michelotti, a member of the popular faction known as the Raspanti, made his triumphant entry into Perugia, and the general council appointed him as the "knight of the people" of Perugia and the "general captain" of the militias. A special commission of twenty-five citizens was tasked with banishing one hundred and fifty noblemen, while Biordo decided the return of noble individuals who were not considered guilty of sedition. Among the exiled noblemen was Braccio da Montone, one of the most skilled military leaders of the time, who vowed not to seek "any pact or agreement with the Raspanti of Perugia."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

It was precisely against the exiled nobles, especially Braccio da Montone, the soul and leader of the noble movement in exile, that the government of the Raspanti directed its efforts after the turmoil of 1393. Holding virtually all power, Biordo was recognized as the first "lord of Perugia," even though during his short rule (1393–1398), he left intact the priory and all existing communal institutions, focusing solely on extending his dominion beyond Perugia. After the splendid marriage with Giovanna Orsini, Biordo and his bride took residence in the Porta Sole palace, but on March 10, 1398, Biordo fell victim to a conspiracy orchestrated by Francesco Guidalotti, abbot of San Pietro. In their new residence, Michelotti was stabbed by Giovanni and Annibaldo, brothers of the abbot of San Pietro.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### [Gian Galeazzo Visconti](/wiki/Gian_Galeazzo_Visconti "Gian Galeazzo Visconti") (1400–1402)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Gian Galeazzo Visconti (1400–1402)")]

The death of Michelotti removed an important point of reference, and factions threatened the stability within and outside the walls. Meanwhile, the twenty-five worked diligently to find an institution that could protect the city and alleviate a very high debt. The people's demands for independence were no longer as urgent, and in the 15th century, the common belief was that they had to accept a ruler or master capable of providing the minimum requirements for survival. The choice fell on the Duchy of Milan, and on January 21, 1400, Gian Galeazzo Visconti was proclaimed the lord of Perugia by the voluntary submission of the city. In response to the delegation of the ten representatives that Perugia had sent him, he canceled the debt of the Perugians. His lordship was short-lived, and on October 3, 1402, Visconti passed away.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### [Braccio da Montone](/wiki/Braccio_da_Montone "Braccio da Montone") (1416–1424)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Braccio da Montone (1416–1424)")]

In 1408, Ladislao D'Angiò Durazzo, the King of Naples, successfully captured Perugia and intended to have it administered by [Braccio Fortebracci](/wiki/Braccio_da_Montone "Braccio da Montone"). However, Braccio vehemently opposed this idea and declined the offer. Nonetheless, in 1411, Perugia surrendered to the King of Naples, opting to be ruled by a foreigner rather than a nobleman. Braccio viewed this as a profound betrayal by his fellow citizens and fled. In November 1410, Braccio besieged Perugia but failed to capture it due to the city's resistance. He defeated pursuing troops and terrorized surrounding towns. In April 1416, he returned with a large army and attacked Perugia. After a victory in July, Perugia surrendered, marking the end of the Raspanti government. Braccio ruled moderately. In 1417, he entered Rome and proclaimed himself Defender of the City, later returning to Umbria. After conflicts and military successes, he was appointed Vicar by the Pope. Upon returning to Perugia, he undertook public works. He left for Bologna, returned, and went to Calabria. When denied entry to L'Aquila, he laid siege but faced opposition from the Pope and Queen. A league attacked him near Pescara in 1424, leading to his death. His son later buried him in Perugia with honors. During the rule of Braccio Fortebracci da Montone, significant public works were undertaken, such as Braccio's residence in the square, of which only the loggias remain, or the "Sopramuro," to which Braccio had another series of supporting structures built: the "briglie di Braccio."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### The Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Renaissance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Territorio_Perugino_-_CBT_5882112.jpg/250px-Territorio_Perugino_-_CBT_5882112.jpg)](/wiki/File:Territorio_Perugino_-_CBT_5882112.jpg)

The territory once extended from [Montecastelli](/wiki/Umbertide "Umbertide") in the north to [Marsciano](/wiki/Marsciano "Marsciano") in the south, [Vaiano](/wiki/Vaiano,_Castiglione_del_Lago "Vaiano, Castiglione del Lago") in the west, and [Poggio Sant'Ercolano](/wiki/Gualdo_Tadino "Gualdo Tadino") in the east

#### [Baglioni family](/wiki/Baglioni_family "Baglioni family") (1438–1540)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Baglioni family (1438–1540)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Braccio_Baglioni%2C_Gonfalone_di_Benedetto_Bonfigli.jpg/250px-Braccio_Baglioni%2C_Gonfalone_di_Benedetto_Bonfigli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Braccio_Baglioni,_Gonfalone_di_Benedetto_Bonfigli.jpg)

Braccio Baglioni, Lord of Perugia from 1438 to 1479

During the period 1438–1479, the Baglioni family held a covert lordship over Perugia, which was not characterized by complete control of civic powers. Braccio I Baglioni, leveraging his position as captain of the militias of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and being the nephew of Braccio da Montone, the previous Lord of the City, exerted an influence over Perugia that quickly established its supremacy. During those years, the Umbrian center experienced a period of flourishing growth as the Baglioni implemented a policy of expansion and beautification of the city, including the construction of new roads and palaces.Between 1429 and 1433, the Palazzo dei Priori was expanded and new churches and private chapels were built. The patronage of the Baglioni attracted artists such as Piero della Francesca, Pinturicchio, and Raphael, making Perugia an important artistic center.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Pietro_Perugino_cat52a-1.jpg/250px-Pietro_Perugino_cat52a-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pietro_Perugino_cat52a-1.jpg)

[Pietro Perugino](/wiki/Pietro_Perugino "Pietro Perugino")

During this time, Perugia became a significant hub of the Umbrian Renaissance, marked by the production of the eight panels depicting the life of Saint Bernardino, a collaborative effort probably involving [Pinturicchio](/wiki/Pinturicchio "Pinturicchio"), [Piermatteo d'Amelia](/wiki/Piermatteo_Lauro_de%27_Manfredi_da_Amelia "Piermatteo Lauro de' Manfredi da Amelia"), and the young [Perugino](/wiki/Pietro_Perugino "Pietro Perugino"), among others, commonly referred to as the "1473 workshop."[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Pietro "Perugino" Vannucci](/wiki/Pietro_Perugino "Pietro Perugino") created numerous works in the city, including a cycle of frescoes in the Hall of Audiences of the [Collegio del Cambio](/wiki/Collegio_del_Cambio "Collegio del Cambio").

Additionally, the Baglioni family commissioned the construction of an imposing aristocratic palace as their private residence, of which only the part incorporated into the Rocca Paolina remains today. The palace was decorated by Domenico Veneziano with a painting cycle depicting noble Perugian families and great military leaders of the past.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Giuseppe-rossi_rocca-paolina.jpg/250px-Giuseppe-rossi_rocca-paolina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe-rossi_rocca-paolina.jpg)

[Rocca Paolina](/wiki/Rocca_Paolina "Rocca Paolina"), view of the fortress in a 19th century painting

Following mutual atrocities of the Oddi and the Baglioni families, power was at last concentrated in the Baglioni, who though they had no legal position, defied all other authority, though their bloody internal squabbles culminated in the [Red Wedding](/wiki/Red_Wedding_(Perugia) "Red Wedding (Perugia)") massacre, 14 July 1500.[[24]](#cite_note-touring-24) [Gian Paolo Baglioni](/wiki/Gian_Paolo_Baglioni "Gian Paolo Baglioni") was lured to Rome in 1520 and beheaded by [Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X"); and in 1540, Rodolfo, who had slain a papal legate, was defeated by [Pier Luigi Farnese](/wiki/Pier_Luigi_Farnese "Pier Luigi Farnese"), and the city, captured and plundered by his soldiery, was deprived of its privileges.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Rocca_Paolina.jpg/250px-Rocca_Paolina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rocca_Paolina.jpg)

In the [Rocca Paolina](/wiki/Rocca_Paolina "Rocca Paolina")

A citadel known as the [Rocca Paolina](/wiki/Rocca_Paolina "Rocca Paolina"), after the name of [Pope Paul III](/wiki/Pope_Paul_III "Pope Paul III"), was built, to designs of [Antonio da Sangallo the Younger](/wiki/Antonio_da_Sangallo_the_Younger "Antonio da Sangallo the Younger") "*ad coercendam Perusinorum audaciam*."[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1797, the city was conquered by French troops. On 4 February 1798, the *Tiberina Republic* was formed, with Perugia as capital, and the French [tricolour](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France") as flag. In 1799, the Tiberina Republic merged to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic_(18th_century) "Roman Republic (18th century)").

In 1832, 1838, and 1854, Perugia was hit by earthquakes. Following the collapse of the [Roman republic of 1848–49](/wiki/Roman_Republic_(19th_century) "Roman Republic (19th century)"), when the Rocca was in part demolished,[[24]](#cite_note-touring-24) it was seized in May 1849 by the [Austrians](/wiki/Austria "Austria"). In June 1859, the inhabitants rebelled against the temporal authority of the pope and established a provisional government, but [the insurrection](/wiki/1859_Perugia_uprising "1859 Perugia uprising") was quashed bloodily by [Pius IX's](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX") troops.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In September 1860, the city was united finally, along with the rest of Umbria, as part of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the city suffered only some damage and was liberated by the [British 8th army](/wiki/Eighth_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Eighth Army (United Kingdom)") on 20 June 1944.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Economy")]

Perugia has become famous for [chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate"), mostly because of a single firm, [Perugina](/wiki/Perugina "Perugina"), whose *Baci* ("kisses" in English) are widely exported.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Perugian chocolate is popular in Italy. The company's plant located in San Sisto (Perugia) is the largest of Nestlé's nine sites in Italy.[[30]](#cite_note-30) According to the Nestlé USA official website,[[31]](#cite_note-31) today Baci is the most famous chocolate brand in Italy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city hosts a chocolate festival, [EuroChocolate](/wiki/EuroChocolate "EuroChocolate"), every October.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Perugia_vista_dal_museo_archeologico_nazionale_dell%27Umbria%2C_2023.jpg/250px-Perugia_vista_dal_museo_archeologico_nazionale_dell%27Umbria%2C_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perugia_vista_dal_museo_archeologico_nazionale_dell%27Umbria,_2023.jpg)

Perugia seen from the national archaeological museum of Umbria

Perugia is the capital city of the region of Umbria. Cities' distances from Perugia: Assisi 19 km (12 mi), Siena 102 km (63 mi), Florence 145 km (90 mi), Rome 164 km (102 mi).

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Climate")]

Though Perugia is located in the central part of Italy, the city experiences a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*) similar to much of Northern Italy due to its inland location and the diverse, hilly topography of Umbria. Typically, summers are warm to hot and humid, while winters are cold with occasional snowfall. The climate in this area has mild differences between highs and lows, with adequate rainfall year-round.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2007, there were 163,287 people residing in the urban area of Perugia, located in the province of Perugia, [Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria"), of whom 47.7% were male and 52.3% were female. Minors (children ages 18 and younger) totalled 16.41% of the population compared to pensioners who number 21.51%. This compares with the Italian average of 18.06% (minors) and 19.94% (pensioners). The average age of Perugia residents is 44 compared to the Italian average of 42. In the five years between 2002 and 2007, the population of Perugia grew by 7.86%, while [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") as a whole grew by 3.85%.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit), 90.84% of the population was [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people"). The largest immigrant group came from other [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") countries (particularly from [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") and [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania")): 3.93%, the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"): 2.01%, and [North African](/wiki/North_African "North African"): 1.3%. The majority of inhabitants are [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

Perugia today hosts two main universities, the ancient [Università degli Studi](/wiki/University_of_Perugia "University of Perugia") (University of Perugia) and the [Foreigners University](/wiki/University_for_Foreigners_Perugia "University for Foreigners Perugia") (*Università per Stranieri*). *Stranieri* serves as an Italian language and culture school for students from all over the world.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Other educational institutions are the Perugia Fine Arts Academy "Pietro Vannucci" (founded in 1573), the Perugia Music Conservatory for the study of classical music, and the RAI Public Broadcasting School of Radio-Television Journalism.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The city is also host to the Umbra Institute, an accredited university program for American students studying abroad.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The *Università dei Sapori* (University of Tastes), a National centre for Vocational Education and Training in Food, is located in the city as well.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Churches")]

* *[Cathedral of S. Lorenzo](/wiki/Perugia_Cathedral "Perugia Cathedral")*
* *[San Pietro](/wiki/San_Pietro,_Perugia "San Pietro, Perugia")*: late 16th-century church and abbey.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Vetrata_San_Domenico%2C_Perugia%2C_2023.jpg/250px-Vetrata_San_Domenico%2C_Perugia%2C_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vetrata_San_Domenico,_Perugia,_2023.jpg)

  The stained glass window of the Basilica of San Domenico (1411)

  *[San Domenico](/wiki/San_Domenico,_Perugia "San Domenico, Perugia")*: Basilica church of the Dominican order, building began in 1394 and finished in 1458. Before 1234, this site housed markets and a horse fair. The exterior design attributed to [Giovanni Pisano](/wiki/Giovanni_Pisano "Giovanni Pisano"), while its interior redecorated in Baroque fashion by [Carlo Maderno](/wiki/Carlo_Maderno "Carlo Maderno"). The massive belfry was partially cut around the mid-16th century. The interior hosts the splendid tomb of [Pope Benedict XI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XI "Pope Benedict XI") and a wooden choir from the Renaissance period. The 23 m × 8 m (75 ft × 26 ft) stained glass window of the Basilica of San Domenico is one of the largest in the world.
* *[Sant'Angelo](/wiki/Church_of_Sant%27Angelo,_Perugia "Church of Sant'Angelo, Perugia")*, also called *San Michele Arcangelo*: small paleo-Christian church from the 5th–6th centuries. Sixteen antique columns frame circular layout recalling the Roman church of [Santo Stefano Rotondo](/wiki/Santo_Stefano_Rotondo "Santo Stefano Rotondo").
* *[Sant'Antonio Abate](/w/index.php?title=Sant%27Antonio_Abate_di_Perugia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sant'Antonio Abate di Perugia (page does not exist)")*.
* *[San Bernardino](/w/index.php?title=San_Bernardino,_Perugia&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Bernardino, Perugia (page does not exist)")*: church with façade by [Agostino di Duccio](/wiki/Agostino_di_Duccio "Agostino di Duccio").
* *[San Ercolano](/w/index.php?title=Sant%27Ercolano,_Perugia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sant'Ercolano, Perugia (page does not exist)")*: 14th-century church that resembles a polygonal tower. This church once had two floors. Its upper floor was demolished when the Rocca Paolina was built. Baroque interior decorations commissioned from 1607. The main altar has a sarcophagus found in 1609.
* *[Santa Giuliana](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Giuliana,_Perugia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santa Giuliana, Perugia (page does not exist)")*: church and monastery founded by heir of a female monastery in 1253. In its later years, the church gained a reputation for dissoluteness. Later, the Napoleonic forces turned the church into a granary. Now, the church is a military hospital. The church, with a single nave, bears only traces of 13th century frescoes, which probably used to cover all of the walls. The cloister is a noteworthy example of mid-14th-century Cistercian architecture from [Matteo Gattaponi](/w/index.php?title=Matteo_Gattaponi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matteo Gattaponi (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matteo_Gattaponi "it:Matteo Gattaponi")]. The upper part of the *[campanile](/wiki/Campanile "Campanile")* is from the 13th century.
* *[San Bevignate](/wiki/San_Bevignate "San Bevignate")*: church of the [Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar").

### Secular buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Secular buildings")]

* The [Palazzo dei Priori](/wiki/Palazzo_dei_Priori "Palazzo dei Priori") (Town Hall, encompassing the [Collegio del Cambio](/wiki/Collegio_del_Cambio "Collegio del Cambio"), Collegio della Mercanzia, and Galleria Nazionale), one of Italy's greatest buildings.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The Collegio del Cambio has frescoes by [Pietro Perugino](/wiki/Pietro_Perugino "Pietro Perugino"), while the Collegio della Mercanzia has a fine later 14th century wooden interior.
  + [Galleria Nazionale dell'Umbria](/wiki/Galleria_Nazionale_dell%27Umbria "Galleria Nazionale dell'Umbria"), the National Gallery of Umbrian art in Middle Ages and Renaissance (it includes works by Duccio, [Piero della Francesca](/wiki/Piero_della_Francesca "Piero della Francesca"), [Beato Angelico](/wiki/Beato_Angelico "Beato Angelico"), [Perugino](/wiki/Perugino "Perugino"))
* *[Fontana Maggiore](/wiki/Fontana_Maggiore "Fontana Maggiore")*, a medieval fountain designed by Fra Bevignate and sculpted by [Nicola](/wiki/Nicola_Pisano "Nicola Pisano") and [Giovanni Pisano](/wiki/Giovanni_Pisano "Giovanni Pisano").

### Medieval towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Medieval towers")]

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Torre_degli_Sciri%2C_Perugia.jpg/250px-Torre_degli_Sciri%2C_Perugia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torre_degli_Sciri,_Perugia.jpg)

  Torre degli Sciri

  [Sciri's tower](/wiki/Torre_degli_Sciri "Torre degli Sciri")[[45]](#cite_note-45) (XIII century), the most well known among the remaining towers in Perugia. It takes its name from the Sciri family and is the only control tower on the noble blocks that still stands. Standing at 42 meters tall and featuring a square shape, it has recently undergone a renovation that has restored its ancient hues, including the distinctive color of the building stone. In 1680, the tower was incorporated into the Convent of the Franciscan Tertiaries of Sister Lucia.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Torre_del_Cassero_o_Porta_Sant%27Angelo_%28Perugia%29.jpg/250px-Torre_del_Cassero_o_Porta_Sant%27Angelo_%28Perugia%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torre_del_Cassero_o_Porta_Sant%27Angelo_(Perugia).jpg)

  Torre del Cassero di Porta Sant'Angelo

Like the town itself, the gate derives its name from the ancient early Christian church dedicated to Saint Michael the Archangel."

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Collegio_del_cambio%2C_Perugia_2023.jpg/250px-Collegio_del_cambio%2C_Perugia_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Collegio_del_cambio,_Perugia_2023.jpg)

[Collegio del Cambio](/wiki/Collegio_del_Cambio "Collegio del Cambio")

Perugia has had a rich tradition of art and artists. The [Early Renaissance](/wiki/Early_Renaissance "Early Renaissance") painter Pietro Perugino created some of his masterpieces in the Perugia area. The High Renaissance master [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael") was also active in Perugia and painted his famous [Oddi Altar](/wiki/Oddi_Altar_(Raphael) "Oddi Altar (Raphael)") there in 1502–04.

Today, the [Galleria Nazionale dell'Umbria](/wiki/Galleria_Nazionale_dell%27Umbria "Galleria Nazionale dell'Umbria") in Perugia houses a number of masterpieces, including the *[Madonna with Child and six Angels](/wiki/Madonna_with_Child_and_six_Angels_(Duccio) "Madonna with Child and six Angels (Duccio)")*, which represents the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") [Marian art](/wiki/Marian_art_in_the_Catholic_Church "Marian art in the Catholic Church") of [Duccio](/wiki/Duccio "Duccio"). And the private [Art collection of Fondazione Cassa di Risparmio di Perugia](/wiki/Art_collection_of_Fondazione_Cassa_di_Risparmio_di_Perugia "Art collection of Fondazione Cassa di Risparmio di Perugia") has two separate locations.

The [Collegio del Cambio](/wiki/Collegio_del_Cambio "Collegio del Cambio") is an extremely well preserved representation of a Renaissance building and houses a magnificent Pietro Perugino fresco.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The newly re-opened Academy of Fine Arts has a small but impressive plaster casts gallery and Perugian paintings and drawings from the 16th century on.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Stadio_renato_curi_Perugia.jpg/250px-Stadio_renato_curi_Perugia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadio_renato_curi_Perugia.jpg)

[AC Perugia Calcio](/wiki/AC_Perugia_Calcio "AC Perugia Calcio") play at the 28,000-seater [Stadio Renato Curi](/wiki/Stadio_Renato_Curi "Stadio Renato Curi").

[AC Perugia Calcio](/wiki/AC_Perugia_Calcio "AC Perugia Calcio") is the main football club in the city, playing in Italy's third-highest division [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C"). The club plays at the 28,000-seat [Stadio Renato Curi](/wiki/Stadio_Renato_Curi "Stadio Renato Curi"), named after a former player who died during a match. From 1983 to 2001, the stadium held four matches for the [Italy national football team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Perugia has two [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") teams: L.R.N. Perugia and Gryphus. The team of LRN Perugia is currently in SERIE B (second-highest division) and the Gryphus team is in the SERIE C (the third highest) division. The L.R.N Perugia has also a women's water polo team which is also playing in the division of SERIE B.

[Sir Safety Umbria Volley](/wiki/Sir_Safety_Umbria_Volley "Sir Safety Umbria Volley"), in English Sir Sicoma Colussi Perugia, is an Italian [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") club, playing at the top level of the Italian Volleyball League. They won their first Italian championship in 2018. Notable players include [Luciano de Cecco](/wiki/Luciano_de_Cecco "Luciano de Cecco") of Argentina, [Aleksandar Atanasijević](/wiki/Aleksandar_Atanasijevi%C4%87 "Aleksandar Atanasijević") of Serbia, and [Wilfredo Leon](/wiki/Wilfredo_Leon "Wilfredo Leon") of Poland.

The martial arts in Perugia have been present since the sixties with Chinese techniques, followed by judo. Later there were karate contact (later called kickboxing), karate, taijiquan, jūjutsu, kendo, aikido, taekwondo and, in recent years, krav maga has also arrived.

In 2014 Jessica Scricciolo, under the Ju-Jitsu Sports Group Perugia, won the title of World Champion in the Fighting System speciality, 55 kg. In March 2015 at the World Championship of Greece (J.J.I.F.) Andrea Calzon' (Ju-Jitsu Sports Group Perugia) won the gold medal in the Ne-Waza (U21.56 kg) and a bronze medal in the Fighting System.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Perugia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Minimetro_03.jpg/250px-Minimetro_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minimetro_03.jpg)

[Minimetrò](/wiki/Minimetr%C3%B2 "Minimetrò")

An electric tramway operated in Perugia from 1901 until 1940. It was decommissioned in favour of buses, and since 1943 trolley buses – the latter were in service until 1975.

Two elevators were established since 1971:

* Mercato Coperto (Parking) – Terrazza Mercato Coperto
* Galleria Kennedy – Mercato Coperto (Pincetto)

This was followed by public escalators:

* Rocca Paolina: Piazza Partigiani – Piazza Italia (1983)
* Cupa-Pellini: Piazzale della Cupa – Via dei Priori (1989)
* Piazzale Europa – Piazzale Bellucci (1993)
* Piazzale Bellucci – Corso Cavour (1993)
* Minimetrò: Pincetto – Piazza Matteotti (2008)

Since 1971 Perugia has taken several measures against car traffic, when the first traffic restriction zone was implemented. These zones were expanded over time and at certain hours of the day driving is forbidden in the city centre. Large parking lots are provided in the lower town, from where the city can be reached via public transport.

Since 2008, an [automated people mover](/wiki/Automated_people_mover "Automated people mover") called [Minimetrò](/wiki/Minimetr%C3%B2 "Minimetrò") has also been in operation. It has seven stations, with one terminal at a large parking lot, and the other in the city centre.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Stazione-perugia.jpg/250px-Stazione-perugia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stazione-perugia.jpg)

The main [railway station](/wiki/Railway_station "Railway station") of Perugia: [Perugia Fontivegge](/wiki/Perugia_railway_station "Perugia railway station")

[Perugia railway station](/wiki/Perugia_railway_station "Perugia railway station"), also known as Perugia Fontivegge, was opened in 1866. It forms part of the [Foligno–Terontola railway](/wiki/Foligno%E2%80%93Terontola_railway "Foligno–Terontola railway"), which also links [Florence](/wiki/Firenze_Santa_Maria_Novella_railway_station "Firenze Santa Maria Novella railway station") with [Rome](/wiki/Roma_Termini_railway_station "Roma Termini railway station"). The station is situated at Piazza [Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Vittorio_Veneto "Vittorio Veneto"), in the heavily populated district of Fontivegge, about 3 kilometres (1.9 mi) southwest of the city centre.

[Perugia San Francesco d'Assisi – Umbria International Airport](/wiki/Perugia_San_Francesco_d%27Assisi_%E2%80%93_Umbria_International_Airport "Perugia San Francesco d'Assisi – Umbria International Airport") is located 12 kilometres (7.5 mi) outside the city.

From the bus station there has been a daily connection of [ITA Airways](/wiki/ITA_Airways "ITA Airways") from 1 December 2022, by bus, to and from [Rome Fiumicino Airport](/wiki/Rome_Fiumicino_Airport "Rome Fiumicino Airport"), allowing a connection with the airline's hub.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
